summary: McKenzie highlights his brother's blog optimization experiments for a superhero novel site, noting that clearer header art communicating the blog's unique selling proposition increased reader retention by 25%. He connects this to his own experiments with evergreen content on Bingo Card Creator, where reducing the number of free bingo cards offered on a page paradoxically increased downloads, doubled time on site, and shifted conversions from low-value card downloads to higher-value trial downloads.
key_points: Making a blog's unique selling proposition visually clear in header art can increase reader retention by 25%
Rewriting and improving old evergreen content pays dividends for any online business
Reducing the amount of free content offered can paradoxically increase engagement and conversion quality
Shifting users from low-value conversions (pennies) to higher-value ones (trial downloads worth 60 cents) matters more than raw conversion volume
money_quotes: Rewriting old evergreen content to make it better bears some fruits.
I can vouch for my brother's finding on "adding by subtracting" — decreasing the number of free bingo cards offered on one page greatly increased the percentage of visitors who downloaded.
It also drove many folks from a conversion to a card (low value — measured in pennies) to a conversion to the trial (much higher value — 60 cents or so).
This has apparently caused about 25% more readers to stick around and actually keep reading when they're drawn into his copious linkbaiting efforts.
I do wee little experiments with my most popular pages quite frequently to see what I can do to increase their popularity and also increase the amount of value they pump into the rest of my business.
key_insight: Offering less free content can increase both engagement and the quality of conversions by pushing users toward higher-value actions.
